policy_statement: Implement the Tarsheed campaign to reduce per-capita consumption of electricity and water (completed 2007).
mechanism_description: A conservation campaign shifts consumer behavior and practices to reduce per-capita electricity and water consumption, lowering associated energy use and emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Human behavior change → ↓ electricity & water demand → ↓ energy generation and water-system energy use → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed activity/system: Household and commercial end-use practices (e.g., turning off lights/AC, reducing hot-water use, fixing leaks, efficient irrigation habits) that lower per-capita electricity and water consumption.  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced demand decreases electricity generation (often fossil-based) and reduces energy required for water abstraction, desalination (if applicable), pumping, distribution, and wastewater treatment.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂e emissions from the power sector and water utility operations due to reduced energy throughput.
dominant_pathway_test: Reducing per-capita electricity and water use most strongly affects climate by lowering energy demand and the associated GHG emissions from power generation and water system operations.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Behavior-change campaigns reduce electricity and water demand, which reduces energy use and thus GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While reduced water use can improve drought resilience (adaptation) and improve system efficiency (resource efficiency framing), the single biggest climate-relevant effect common to all such campaigns is avoided emissions from reduced energy demand.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a campaign is framed solely as water security (no material energy/GHG impact), adaptation might dominate; however the canonical mechanism explicitly includes reduced electricity use and emissions, keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy matching “conservation campaign → per-capita energy and water use reduction” will, by definition, act through demand reduction to lower energy use and associated emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Any more specific subtype (e.g., “water-energy nexus,” “behavior change,” “demand-side management,” or adaptation co-benefits) must be assigned based on policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In highly decarbonized grids, the emissions benefit from electricity savings may be smaller and water-security/adaptation benefits could become relatively more important; in desalination-heavy systems, the mitigation benefit is typically larger due to high electricity intensity of water supply.
key_indicators: “Tarsheed campaign”, “Initiative”, “reduce the percapita consumption of electricity and water”, “completed in 2007”
co_benefits: Improved water security and reduced desalination burden; reduced peak-load stress during extreme heat; potential household cost savings.
instance_edge_case_notes: Campaign details (e.g., whether it included rebates, audits, or targets) are not specified; classified as information/disclosure based on the explicit “campaign/initiative” framing focused on reducing consumption.
